summaries:
The team has initiated an authentication system rebuild under the tracking label `auth-rebuild-design`, structured in four phases: schema design, token issuance, validation middleware, and cutover. The core architectural decision was to adopt JWT with HS256 signing over OAuth-only, driven by the need for stateless validation at the edge to support a peak load of 12k requests per second without session-store roundtrips. The token strategy uses 15-minute access tokens paired with 7-day refresh tokens, with the JWT signing key stored in SSM Parameter Store at /molt/prod/jwt-signing-key, encrypted with KMS and rotated quarterly.

The existing authentication endpoint is /api/v1/auth/login, which currently issues a cookie named molt_session with HttpOnly and SameSite=Lax attributes. The JWT payload includes standard claims (sub for user_id, iss as auth.example.com, aud as api.example.com, exp, iat, jti as UUID) plus custom claims for amr (set to ['mfa'] when multi-factor authentication is passed) and tier (free/pro/enterprise). The SameSite=Lax setting was chosen because the application doesn't rely on cross-site POSTs for authentication flows, making the stricter Strict policy unnecessary.

Refresh token security employs a sliding window rotation strategy where each refresh operation issues a new refresh token and blacklists the old one in a Redis short-TTL set. The blacklist uses the existing molt-cache-prod Redis cluster (cluster mode, 3 shards, Multi-AZ) under the keyspace auth:refresh:blacklist:* with an 8-day TTL. When refresh operations fail, the system returns a 401 status with a WWW-Authenticate: Bearer error="invalid_token" header, prompting the client to redirect to /login. If Redis becomes unavailable, the blacklist check fails open (accepting the refresh token) while triggering a paging alert, with the option to switch to an in-memory fallback for brief outages. The rate-limit store also uses molt-cache-prod, which is confirmed to be highly available with Multi-AZ deployment.

Logging follows a tiered approach: INFO level for successful authentication paths, WARN for refresh failures, and ERROR for signing failures, all in JSON-structured format. Audit logs are retained for 90 days hot in CloudWatch and 7 years cold in S3 Glacier to meet compliance requirements. Error messages support internationalization with keys for English, Spanish, French, and Japanese. The testing strategy includes unit tests on validation middleware achieving 95% coverage and end-to-end tests via Playwright covering the complete login, refresh, and revoke flow. A known Safari 16 issue with HttpOnly cookies in private mode is addressed with a fallback to sessionStorage accompanied by a security warning in the UI. A penetration test is planned with Cobalt once a staging deployment with the full token flow is available, with one outstanding security review item already resolved regarding the high availability of the rate-limit Redis store.

The team has moved from design into implementation of the authentication token issuance system, renaming the thread from `auth-rebuild-design` to `auth-token-issuance` to reflect this transition. The schema work is complete, and development has begun on the new token issuance endpoint at /api/v2/auth/token. This endpoint accepts POST requests with username, password, and mfa_code, returning both access and refresh tokens while setting a molt_session cookie.

A critical security decision was made to support TOTP-only multi-factor authentication via authenticator apps, explicitly excluding SMS-based MFA due to interception risks and US carrier reliability concerns for a financial application. For SSO users, the system will trust the SAML assertion's amr claim to indicate that MFA has already been enforced at the identity provider layer, avoiding redundant prompts. The JWT signing infrastructure has been updated with a new key at /molt/prod/jwt-signing-key-v2, encrypted with KMS multi-region key mrk-1234abcd5678efgh, while the old key is being retained for a seven-day grace period.

The JWT payload structure includes sub (user_id), iss (auth.example.com), aud (api.example.com), exp, iat, jti (UUID), amr (['mfa'] when MFA passed), and tier (free/pro/enterprise). Token revocation is handled via POST /api/v2/auth/revoke, which adds the JTI to a Redis blacklist and revokes the entire refresh token chain. When refresh operations fail, the system returns a 401 status with a WWW-Authenticate: Bearer error="invalid_token" header, triggering a client-side redirect to /login. The molt_session cookie uses SameSite=Lax, deemed sufficient since no critical cross-site POST operations exist for authentication.

Comprehensive audit logging captures the amr claim value, IP address, user agent, and success/failure status to the CloudWatch log group /molt/prod/auth-audit. Logs are retained for 90 days hot in CloudWatch and seven years cold in S3 Glacier to meet compliance requirements. Logging levels are structured as INFO for success paths, WARN for refresh failures, and ERROR for signing failures, all in JSON format. Error messages support internationalization with keys for English, Spanish, French, and Japanese. A known Safari 16 issue with HttpOnly cookies in private mode is addressed with a sessionStorage fallback accompanied by a security warning in the UI.

The system includes resilience measures for Redis outages: if the refresh token blacklist store becomes unavailable, the system fails open by accepting refresh tokens while triggering a paging alert, with the option to switch to an in-memory fallback for short outages. The rate-limit Redis store is confirmed to be highly available, backed by molt-cache-prod running in a multi-AZ configuration. Testing coverage includes unit tests on validation middleware achieving 95% coverage and end-to-end Playwright tests covering the complete login, refresh, and revoke flow. All security review items have been addressed, with the rate-limit store's high availability confirmed as the final outstanding concern.

The conversation covers the completion of token issuance work and the subsequent split into two parallel threads: auth-validation-middleware and auth-mfa-flow. The team selected jose as the JWT validation library over jsonwebtoken due to its superior TypeScript types and native use of node:crypto without additional dependencies. CORS configuration allows origins from https://app.example.com and https://admin.example.com with credentials enabled to support cookie-based flows.

Rate limiting on the token endpoint is set at 60 requests per minute per IP with a burst allowance of 100, implemented using express-rate-limit with a Redis store running on molt-cache-prod. The system emits three key metrics to Datadog with the env:prod tag: auth.token.issued.count, auth.token.revoked.count, and auth.mfa.failure.count. The validation middleware has reached feature-complete status in staging and is awaiting penetration testing, while the MFA flow is active with completed UI hooks and a live backend QR-code endpoint, though the TOTP secret rotation flow remains pending.

Cobalt has been engaged for penetration testing with a 5-day window starting Monday, involving two testers. When refresh tokens fail, the system returns a 401 status with a WWW-Authenticate: Bearer error="invalid_token" header, triggering a client-side redirect to /login. Logging follows a structured approach with INFO level for success paths, WARN for refresh failures, and ERROR for signing failures, all in JSON format.

The JWT payload structure includes sub (user_id), iss (auth.example.com), aud (api.example.com), exp, iat, jti (UUID), amr (['mfa'] when MFA passed), and tier (free/pro/enterprise). Cookies use SameSite=Lax rather than Strict since there are no cross-site POSTs critical to authentication. A known Safari 16 issue with HttpOnly cookies in private mode is addressed through a fallback to sessionStorage, accompanied by a security warning in the UI.

Testing coverage includes unit tests on the validation middleware achieving 95% coverage and end-to-end Playwright tests covering the complete login, refresh, and revoke flow. Error messages support internationalization with keys for English, Spanish, French, and Japanese. If Redis becomes unavailable, the refresh token blacklist fails open (accepting refresh tokens) while triggering a paging alert, with the option to switch to an in-memory fallback for brief outages. Audit logs are retained for 90 days hot in CloudWatch and 7 years cold in S3 Glacier to meet compliance requirements. The single open security review item—confirming high availability of the rate-limit Redis store—has been resolved, as molt-cache-prod is confirmed to be multi-AZ.

The conversation covers the final stages of an authentication system v2 launch and the deprecation of v1. Version 2 went GA yesterday, with a deprecation banner now live for v1 users and a sunset date set for 2026-08-01. A recent pen-test identified one medium-severity CSRF vulnerability on the /api/v2/auth/revoke endpoint, which has already been patched using a double-submit cookie pattern. A new thread called auth-deprecation-comms was created to handle customer-facing communications, including drafting emails and changelog posts for the v1 deprecation.

The technical implementation details are well-defined. JWT tokens include standard claims like sub (user_id), iss (auth.example.com), aud (api.example.com), exp, iat, and jti (UUID), plus custom fields for amr (set to ['mfa'] when MFA is passed) and tier (free/pro/enterprise). Refresh token failures return a 401 status with a WWW-Authenticate: Bearer error="invalid_token" header, triggering a client-side redirect to /login. Cookies use SameSite=Lax, which is sufficient since there are no critical cross-site POST operations. A known Safari 16 issue with HttpOnly cookies in private mode is handled via a fallback to sessionStorage, accompanied by a security warning in the UI.

Logging follows a tiered approach: INFO for successful operations, WARN for refresh failures, and ERROR for signing failures, all in JSON-structured format. Testing coverage is robust, with unit tests achieving 95% coverage on validation middleware and end-to-end Playwright tests covering the complete login, refresh, and revoke flows. Error messages support internationalization with keys for English, Spanish, French, and Japanese.

Infrastructure resilience is addressed through several mechanisms. The refresh token blacklist uses Redis, and if Redis goes down, the system fails open (accepting refresh tokens) while triggering a paging alert; an in-memory fallback is available for short outages. Audit logs are retained for 90 days hot in CloudWatch and 7 years cold in S3 Glacier to meet compliance requirements. The rate-limit Redis store is backed by molt-cache-prod, which is multi-AZ and highly available, resolving the one outstanding security review item.

metas.summary:
This session focused on designing a JWT-based authentication rebuild for a high-throughput API (12k rps peak). The core architectural decision was to use JWT with HS256 signing rather than OAuth-only, driven by the need for stateless validation at the edge cache layer. Token lifetimes were set at 15 minutes for access tokens and 7 days for refresh tokens, with a sliding-window refresh strategy that issues new refresh tokens on each refresh and blacklists old ones in Redis.

The implementation leverages existing infrastructure: the JWT signing key lives in SSM Parameter Store (`/molt/prod/jwt-signing-key`), encrypted with KMS and rotated quarterly. The refresh token blacklist uses a dedicated keyspace (`auth:refresh:blacklist:*`) in the existing `molt-cache-prod` Redis cluster (cluster mode, 3 shards, Multi-AZ) with an 8-day TTL. If Redis goes down, the system fails open (accepts refresh tokens) but triggers a paging alert, with an option to fall back to in-memory storage for short outages. The current login endpoint is `/api/v1/auth/login`, and the session cookie is named `molt_session` with HttpOnly and SameSite=Lax attributes.

The JWT payload includes standard claims (sub, iss, aud, exp, iat, jti) plus custom fields for MFA status (amr) and user tier (free/pro/enterprise). Issuer is `auth.example.com` and audience is `api.example.com`. Error handling returns 401 with `WWW-Authenticate: Bearer error="invalid_token"` headers on refresh failures, prompting client-side redirect to `/login`. Logging is JSON-structured with INFO for success, WARN for refresh failures, and ERROR for signing failures. Audit logs are retained 90 days hot in CloudWatch and 7 years cold in S3 Glacier for compliance.

Browser compatibility includes a Safari 16 workaround for HttpOnly cookie issues in private mode, falling back to sessionStorage with a security warning. Error messages support i18n in English, Spanish, French, and Japanese. Testing coverage includes 95% unit test coverage on validation middleware and end-to-end Playwright tests for the full login/refresh/revoke flow. A pen-test engagement with Cobalt is planned once staging deployment is complete. The rate-limit Redis store was confirmed to be HA (backed by the same multi-AZ `molt-cache-prod` cluster), closing the final open security review item.

metas.summary:
This session focused on implementing the JWT token issuance endpoint at `/api/v2/auth/token`, which accepts POST requests with username, password, and optional MFA code, returning both access and refresh tokens while setting the `molt_session` cookie. The thread was renamed from `auth-rebuild-design` to `auth-token-issuance` to reflect the shift from design to implementation. A critical MFA decision was made: the system will support TOTP via authenticator apps only, with no SMS fallback due to interception risks and carrier reliability concerns for a financial application.

The JWT signing key was rotated to a new multi-region KMS key (`mrk-1234abcd5678efgh`), with the new parameter path `/molt/prod/jwt-signing-key-v2`. The old key is retained for a 7-day grace period. For SSO users, MFA is handled at the IdP layer — the system trusts the SAML assertion's `amr` claim to indicate MFA completion and avoids double-prompting. All authentication events, including the `amr` value, IP, user agent, and success/failure status, are logged to the CloudWatch log group `/molt/prod/auth-audit`.

A token revocation endpoint was implemented at `POST /api/v2/auth/revoke`, which adds the JWT's `jti` to the Redis blacklist and revokes the entire refresh token chain. The refresh failure handling, logging levels, JWT payload structure, cookie attributes, browser compatibility workarounds, test coverage, internationalization support, Redis failover strategy, and audit log retention policies remain unchanged from the previous compaction. The final security review item — confirming the rate-limit Redis store is HA — was closed, as it's backed by the same multi-AZ `molt-cache-prod` cluster.

metas.summary:
This session focused on implementing the JWT validation middleware and polishing the MFA flow, splitting the previous `auth-token-issuance` thread into two new sub-threads. The validation middleware is now feature-complete in staging and uses the `jose` npm library (chosen over `jsonwebtoken` for better TypeScript types and native node:crypto support). CORS is configured to allow `https://app.example.com` and `https://admin.example.com` with credentials enabled for cookie flow. Rate limiting on the token endpoint is set at 60 requests per minute per IP with a burst of 100, implemented via `express-rate-limit` backed by the `molt-cache-prod` Redis cluster.

The MFA flow is active with UI hooks complete and a live QR-code endpoint, pending implementation of the TOTP secret rotation flow. A pen-test engagement with Cobalt is scheduled for a 5-day window starting Monday with two testers. Metrics are being emitted to Datadog (`auth.token.issued.count`, `auth.token.revoked.count`, `auth.mfa.failure.count`) with the `env:prod` tag.

All previously established patterns remain unchanged: refresh failures return 401 with `WWW-Authenticate: Bearer error="invalid_token"` headers, logging uses INFO/WARN/ERROR levels with JSON structure, JWT payload includes standard claims plus `amr` and `tier`, cookies use `SameSite=Lax`, Safari 16 private mode has a sessionStorage fallback with security warning, test coverage is 95% on validation middleware with Playwright e2e tests, error messages support English/Spanish/French/Japanese, Redis failover falls open with paging alerts, and audit logs retain 90 days hot in CloudWatch and 7 years cold in S3 Glacier. The final security review item (confirming rate-limit Redis HA) was closed as it's backed by the multi-AZ `molt-cache-prod` cluster.

metas.summary:
This session focused on post-GA activities following the v2 auth system launch. The Cobalt pen-test report identified one medium-severity CSRF vulnerability on the `/api/v2/auth/revoke` endpoint, which was immediately patched using a double-submit cookie pattern. The v2 API was declared GA as of yesterday, with the v1 deprecation banner now live and a sunset date set for August 1, 2026. A new thread was opened to handle customer-facing communications around the v1 deprecation, including drafting the customer email and changelog post.

The remainder of the session consisted of repeated queries confirming existing implementation details, all of which remain unchanged from previous compactions: refresh failure handling returns 401 with `WWW-Authenticate: Bearer error="invalid_token"` headers, logging uses INFO/WARN/ERROR levels with JSON structure, JWT payload includes standard claims plus `amr` and `tier`, cookies use `SameSite=Lax`, Safari 16 private mode has a sessionStorage fallback with security warning, test coverage is 95% on validation middleware with Playwright e2e tests, error messages support English/Spanish/French/Japanese, Redis failover falls open with paging alerts, and audit logs retain 90 days hot in CloudWatch and 7 years cold in S3 Glacier. The rate-limit Redis HA confirmation closed the final security review item.

All previously established patterns and infrastructure remain in place: the `jose` library for JWT validation, CORS configuration for `https://app.example.com` and `https://admin.example.com`, rate limiting at 60 req/min per IP with burst 100 via `express-rate-limit`, Datadog metrics (`auth.token.issued.count`, `auth.token.revoked.count`, `auth.mfa.failure.count`), MFA flow with QR-code endpoint, multi-region KMS key (`mrk-1234abcd5678efgh`) for JWT signing, and the `molt-cache-prod` Redis cluster for blacklist and rate-limiting.
